College: College of Arts and Letters — Humanities
Description: Interdisciplinary study of the African American experience, Africa, and the African Diaspora — race, politics, theology, education, and history across the Africana world.
Interdisciplinary study of U.S. culture and society — courses range from 'The American West' and 'American Capitalism' to 'Race and Popular Culture' and 'Catholics in America.'
Arabic language taught by native speakers alongside Arabic literature, history, civilization, culture, and religion. Housed in the Department of Classics.
Greek and Latin languages with Greek and Roman literature, history, art, religion, philosophy, and archaeology of the Greco-Roman world.
Liberal-arts-oriented computer science combining significant CSE coursework with a required 15-credit cognate area in an Arts and Letters discipline. Housed in A&L.
Literature across periods, forms, and genres with small classes (15–17 students) and strong creative writing offerings in fiction, poetry, nonfiction, and playwriting/screenwriting.
Scholarly study of film/TV/theatre with hands-on production — cinematography, lighting, set design, acting, sound design, animation — and directing/writing tracks.
French language and the literatures, cultures, politics, and societies of the French-speaking world. Major, supplementary major, or minor available.

Two concentrations — Language and Literature (deep immersion with an honors track) and German Studies (broader interdisciplinary program). Supplementary major and international economics track available.
Interdisciplinary survey of Greek and Roman literature and culture from the Late Bronze Age to the disintegration of the Roman Empire — primarily through translation.
The human past from antiquity to the present across every region and theme, with strong emphasis on context, perspective, and historical argumentation.
Economics combined with advanced language training in Arabic, Chinese, French, German, Italian, Japanese, Russian, or Spanish, with study abroad and overseas internship opportunities.
Two tracks — Literature and Culture (language and literary immersion) and Italian Studies (interdisciplinary). A Year in Rome through Notre Dame's Rome Global Gateway is integral.
Japanese language from beginner through advanced levels with literature, culture, and intensive summer language study abroad. Major, supplementary major, and international economics track.
Interdisciplinary study of the U.S. Latino population through the Institute for Latino Studies, including community-based learning and summer programs in Chicago, LA, DC, and Puerto Rico.
Rigorous A&L-housed honors mathematics program emphasizing how and why mathematics works — preparation for careers requiring strong analytic and quantitative skills.
Interdisciplinary major drawing from twelve disciplines around a student-chosen concentration. Requires a Medieval Studies seminar with a substantial research paper.
Music theory, history, and ethnomusicology with academic and performance concentrations. Honors option for graduate-bound students; may be paired with any other major.
Core in Ancient & Medieval Philosophy, Modern Philosophy, and Formal Logic plus five upper-level seminars (10–12 students) across ethics, philosophy of science, feminism, and history of philosophy.
Joint degree completing requirements in both Philosophy and Theology departments — engaging the intersection of systematic philosophy and scriptural/theological traditions.
Integrated great-books curriculum of small Socratic seminars and tutorials spanning the arts, history, literature, natural science, philosophy, politics, and theology.
Cross-cultural major in two Romance languages and literatures (French, Italian, or Spanish) — applicable to international law, business, communications, fine arts, and diplomacy.
Russian language and culture from beginner through advanced levels. Major, supplementary major, international economics track with Russian concentration, and minor.
Spanish language, literature, and culture across Spain, Latin America, the Caribbean, and the U.S. — preparation for careers requiring Spanish-language and cross-cultural competence.

Required core in Scripture and Christian Tradition followed by wide-ranging electives across ancient Judaism, environmental ethics, Catholic dialogue with Islam, and beyond — 200+ majors, 60+ faculty.

College: College of Arts and Letters — Social Sciences
Description: Anthropology with honors track and two concentrations — Health & Culture (medical anthropology, bioanthropology, STS) and Business Anthropology (global trade, cultural marketing, design anthropology).
Liberal-arts economics with strong quantitative, analytical, and communications training. Includes Development Economics, Health Economics, and Economics of Catholic Social Teaching.
Interdisciplinary supplementary major (paired with a primary major) examining the role of education in society. Prepares students for teaching, educational research, nonprofits, and policy.
Interdisciplinary analysis of gender and sexuality in diverse contexts with 60+ affiliated faculty across 22 areas. Strong focus on social justice and community engagement.
Comprehensive coverage of American politics, international relations, comparative politics, and political theory with 45+ faculty — preparation for law, policy, and graduate study.
Brain, mind, and human behavior — preparation for medicine, health professions, graduate study, business, and the private sector. The B.A. in Neuroscience and Behavior is listed separately.

Empirical analysis of social structures and human behavior — quantitative methods, critical thinking, cultural competence, and applications across business, medicine, education, data science, and law.

College: College of Arts and Letters — Arts
Description: Art and architecture of world cultures across the present and past in their political, economic, scientific, and religious contexts — visual analysis, critical thinking, and analytical writing.

Human-centered design across traditional and contemporary media — concentrations in Industrial Design (UX, health care, product, automation) or Visual Communication Design (packaging, UX/UI, data visualization).
Studio practice in aesthetics, composition, critical skills, and both traditional and digital techniques. Interdisciplinary curriculum across a range of media.

College: College of Arts and Letters — Interdisciplinary & Pre-Professional
Description: Supplementary major (paired with a primary A&L major) for students intending health professions — combines A&L breadth with prerequisite science coursework for medical, dental, and other health programs.

Interdisciplinary nervous-systems major (B.A. version housed in A&L; B.S. version in Science). Draws on biology, chemistry, computer science, engineering, linguistics, math, philosophy, physics, and psychology.

Majors: Applied and Computational Mathematics and Statistics (ACMS) (B.S.)
Description: Modeling, simulation, and data analysis for problems across business, engineering, the natural sciences, and social sciences. Preparation for technical fields and quantitative graduate study.
Rigorous chemistry training combined with biological macromolecules and metabolic processes. Smaller classes shared with chemistry majors in early years; strong undergraduate research emphasis.

Majors: Biochemistry with a Concentration in Neuroscience (B.S.)
Description: Biochemistry variant adding a neuroscience concentration — molecular and biochemical underpinnings of nervous-system function. Strong fit for med school or neuroscience graduate research.
Flexible major with a core curriculum and eight specialized tracks for depth — from aquatic ecology to molecular biology — with close mentorship and undergraduate research opportunities.

The 'central science' — preparation for medical/dental school, graduate research, or chemical industry employment. Classes taught separately from non-majors; 50+ research faculty for undergraduate research.
Chemistry with an integrated computing sequence — molecular modeling, data analysis, and algorithm development. Suitable for computational chemistry, cheminformatics, or life-sciences data science.

Majors: Chemistry with a Concentration in Neuroscience (B.S.)
Description: Chemistry variant focused on the chemical and molecular basis of neural function. Preparation for graduate or professional programs at the intersection of chemistry and neuroscience.

Interdisciplinary integration of physical and biological sciences with focus options including aquatic ecology, geological sciences, entomology, vertebrate zoology, and an Earth Sciences concentration.
Mathematics with programs tailored for life sciences, business, engineering, and social science applications. Rigorous honors track for graduate-bound students; emphasis on undergraduate research.

Interdisciplinary nervous-systems major (B.S. version through Science; B.A. version through A&L) from cellular/molecular to behavioral/systems levels. Flexible curriculum for pre-health or research paths.
Rigorous problem-solving training with research opportunities at Fermilab, Argonne, and CERN. Research in astrophysics, nuclear, particle, condensed matter, biophysics, and atomic physics.
Physics combined with the foundation for medical school — 77 science credits required. Specifically designed for medicine-bound students; no additional concentrations permitted.

64-credit interdisciplinary science major for students preparing for health professions. Allows more breadth in social sciences, humanities, and ethics than traditional pre-med tracks. Established 1960.
Individualized program incorporating the four basic science areas plus a computing sequence — working knowledge of computer languages and current computing technology with a science focus.
Individualized program combining the four basic sciences with secondary-science teaching certification requirements. Education courses taken at Saint Mary's College.
Statistical analysis for data-intensive careers — investment data analysis, online sales pattern identification, actuarial risk, political surveys, and manufacturing performance data.

Strategic Cost Management, Ethics in Accounting, Federal Taxation, and Data Analytics in Accounting. Distinguished by emphasis on data analytics, mining, simulation, and optimization. 98%+ placement rate.

Data-driven analysis of business operations using statistics, predictive analytics, data visualization, R, Python, machine learning, and data management — applicable across technology, consulting, finance, and healthcare.
Investment Theory, Corporate Financial Reporting, and Managerial Economics within an ethical framework. Access to the Clark Lecture Series on banking, credit markets, and economic policy.
Combines technical rigor of computer science with strategic business insight — AI-enabled analysis, data modeling, and technology strategy applied to business and operational challenges.
Marketing Research, Consumer and Organizational Buyer Behavior, and Social Media coursework. Signature senior Advertising Campaigns course has students create and present campaigns for real clients.

Formerly Management Consulting — understanding organizational change, employee/market response, and strategic opportunity. Courses include International Management, Strategic IT and Innovation, and Design Thinking.

Design and operation of aircraft and space vehicles. Specializations available in design and manufacturing, thermal/fluid sciences, bioengineering, solid mechanics, materials, control systems, and computational engineering.
Chemical engineering principles combined with modern data science and computational tools. Concentrations available in Biomolecular Engineering, Energy, or Materials.

Design, construction, and renewal of buildings, bridges, roads, dams, airports, and water/energy systems. Sub-disciplines include structural, water resources, transportation, coastal, construction, and geotechnical engineering.
Electronic devices and computer system architecture/organization — internal CPU workings and the assembly of larger computing systems with hands-on prototyping using modern design tools.
Application of computers to real-world problems with emphasis on algorithms, software engineering, and the design of large, efficient, scalable software systems. (B.A. version separately in A&L.)

Electrical and electronic systems across information/communication, materials and devices, circuits, and algorithms for next-generation computing platforms — applicable across aerospace, automotive, medical, and tech industries.

Fluid dynamics, hydrology, environmental chemistry, and geochemistry within Earth-system context. Includes Dynamic Earth, Geomorphology, Earth Materials/Petrology, Environmental Aquatic Chemistry, and Mineralogy.
Water treatment, contaminated-water remediation, solid/hazardous waste management, air pollution mitigation, and waste-to-value sustainability solutions — addressing food, water, and energy demands.

Design of solutions across energy, transportation, robotics, and medical devices. Elective specializations in aerospace, design and manufacturing, thermal/fluid sciences, bioengineering, solid mechanics, materials, and control systems.

Five-year professional degree emphasizing classical and vernacular architecture within traditional urbanism. Year 1: hand drawing and drafting. Year 2: construction principles. Year 3: full year in Rome studying traditional urbanism. Years 4–5: regionalism, cross-cultural values, and a spring thesis studio.

Interdisciplinary study of contemporary global issues — human-centered approach to policy. Core in Integral Human Development, Global Politics and Policy, Principles of Economics, and Quantitative Methods, with electives, language learning, and a capstone thesis integrating cross-cultural experience.
